narrative:
High pressure is now centered to our east. A southerly flow around it brings a seasonally pleasant Saturday. Clouds increase on Sunday, and light rain or drizzle develops in the afternoon on the east side of the Appalachians with fog. Expect rain and fog Sunday night, especially in the eastern half of the mountain region. Temperatures are cooler Sunday.

Monday will eclipse last Thursday in weather drama. Rain increases across a relatively mild Monday morning. Then look for a line of thunderstorms to roll through, followed by unbelievably colder temperatures. Precipitation changes to snow from west to east during the afternoon. Snow showers continue into Monday night. Wind? You betcha! The March Lion roars.

The "Mix and Match" Special Offer continues on all the Snowman O'Mometer Pint Glass and Mug (or you can long for a 5 Golfball Day with that pint glass as well). Buy one, and get another drinkware item at 40% off.

March Lion Returning

Last Updated Sat March 14 at 11:09AM by Ray Russell

High pressure is now centered to our east. A southerly flow around it brings a seasonally pleasant Saturday. Clouds increase on Sunday, and light rain or drizzle develops in the afternoon on the east side of the Appalachians with fog. Expect rain and fog Sunday night, especially in the eastern half of the mountain region. Temperatures are cooler Sunday.

Monday will eclipse last Thursday in weather drama. Rain increases across a relatively mild Monday morning. Then look for a line of thunderstorms to roll through, followed by unbelievably colder temperatures. Precipitation changes to snow from west to east during the afternoon. Snow showers continue into Monday night. Wind? You betcha! The March Lion roars.

Forecast Discussion

It is an impressive pattern flip, from a Bermuda High summer-like pattern to a sharp Eastern U.S. trough. But an even more impressive change is yet to come as the polar vortex shifts south into central Canada, driving impressive cold into the Eastern U.S.

Today, high pressure has shifted to our east, producing a milder southerly flow across the Southern Appalachians. We have a warmer, much more pleasant day.

A weak front fades out as it moves across our area from the north. Sunday, high pressure centered in New York pushes a cooler, damp wedge down the east side of the Appalachians. Sunday is less warm with increasing clouds. Light rain, drizzle, and fog develop on the eastern edge of the mountain region. That cool wedge expands Sunday night, bringing rain to more of the area with drizzle and fog across most of the mountain region.

Monday brings memorable weather, eclipsing what happened on Thursday. Monday morning has increasing rain and relatively warm temperatures. Then a front roars through from the west with a “bang” (thunderstorm), and temperatures plummet. Precipitation changes to snow from west to east Monday afternoon. Snow showers linger into Monday night. Winds will be impressive. We’ll have more snow Monday than we had Thursday.

A snow flurry may linger into a bitterly cold Tuesday (welcome back from Spring Break). Skies clear by sunset Tuesday, and Tuesday night remains bitterly cold. Winds settle down somewhat Tuesday night.

Wednesday, the arctic high shifts slowly to our east, and we begin a slow warmup. Wednesday afternoon is cold for March, but not as cold as Tuesday. Thursday we get back to seasonally average temperatures. By Friday afternoon, we are back to relatively pleasant weather.

Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters.

Latest Insights
Severe Weather Season Around The Corner

As our March weather has become more changeable and volatile, it's a reminder that severe weather season is almost upon us.

Last week was Severe Weather Awareness Week in North Carolina; This week is Virginia's turn.

While western North Carolina and Southwest Virginia may have less overall severe weather than our surrounding neighbors, we still have our fair share, and it's a great time to review your severe weather emergency/preparedeness procedures ahead of time.

Mar 13, 2026
It is, after all, March....

March Madness rules supreme! After record high temperatures on Wednesday, temperatures at noon today are around 40 degrees colder vs. this time on Wednesday.

Oh, and yeah, it snowed in the mountains this morning!

Expect more wild swings this weekend and early next week.

Mar 12, 2026
Monday Message for March 9, 2026

It's March Madness, weather-style, around western North Carolina. We discuss the ups-and-downs of March weather in this week's edition.
